explicit_text: My grandma, Hiroko Okushima, came to the United States in late 1976 with her American husband. Born in post-war Itoman, Okinawa, she grew up near the Yozadake Sub Air Force Base and was deeply influenced by American culture. She used her sewing skills and ability to speak Japanese to support herself and her family through various jobs in California, Oregon, Hawaii, and North Carolina. Even after retiring, she continues to sew for fun and sells her handmade items in Graham, North Carolina. Her sewing knowledge has been passed down to me, which I cherish deeply.

explicit_text: I know the United States as the place my grandma moved to in late 1976 with her husband, seeking a better life. The American culture she admired growing up in Okinawa influenced her decision to move here. The U.S. became her home where she raised her family and continued her sewing craft.

This exposure made her feel like a black sheep in Japanese society and fueled her dream to move to the United States.

Despite feeling out of place in Japanese society, Okinawa shaped her early life and inspired her to seek a new life in the United States.

explicit_text: After moving to the United States in late 1976, my grandma lived in California where she sewed clothing for Sears and JCPenney. This work allowed her to stay home and manage her duties as a housewife. Although her marriage ended in divorce there, California was an important place where she began building her new life in America.

The sound of her sewing machine in Graham is a comforting reminder of home and her lifelong passion.
